title-group.article-title: Human papillomavirus vaccination among Asian Americans and Pacific Islanders: Scoping review and meta-analysis of descriptive and correlational studies

contrib-group.aff: aCollege of Nursing, Michigan State University, East Lansing, USA
bDepartment of Public Health, College of Medicine, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan
cInstitute of Allied Health Sciences, College of Medicine, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan
dSchool of Nursing, College of Nursing, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan

author-notes.corresp: CONTACT Angela Chia-Chen Chen, PhD, PMHNP-BC chenang6@msu.edu College of Nursing, Michigan State University, 1355 Bogue Street, Room C250, East Lansing, MI 48824.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Despite the proven effectiveness of the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine, vaccination intention and uptake remain low among Asian American and Pacific Islanders (AAPIs). Evidence was synthesized from descriptive and correlational studies that examined HPV vaccination intention and/or uptake among AAPIs in the United States. PubMed, CINAHL, PsycINFO, and Cochrane databases were searched. Risk of bias was assessed using Joanna Briggs Institute checklist. Forty-two studies published between 2010 and 2024 were included across Vietnamese, Korean, Cambodian, Chinese, Hmong, and Asian Indians. Over half of the studies assessed vaccination intention, with reported prevalence between 5%–100%. Approximately one-third of studies reported vaccination uptake between 5%–50%. Meta-analyses showed low HPV vaccine awareness, acceptance, intention, initiation and completion, with completion only 21%. Compared with non-AAPIs, AAPIs were significantly less likely to initiate or complete vaccination. These results underscore persistent disparities and the need for culturally tailored strategies to improve HPV vaccination among AAPIs.

funding-group.funding-statement: This study was supported by the corresponding author (Dr. Chen)’s Endowed Chair funds, which provided salary support for SM, SS, NG, and AF. Co-authors affiliated with institutions in Taiwan did not receive financial support from these funds. The funder had no role in the study design; data collection, analysis, or interpretation; manuscript preparation; or the decision to publish.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: There are approximately 24 million individuals who identify with the Asian Americans and Pacific Islander (AAPI) population in the United States1, making them one of the fastest-growing racial and ethnic groups. The AAPI population encompass diverse subgroups originating from more than 30 countries and speaking over 100 languages.1 Despite their rapid growth, the AAPI population remains underrepresented in public health research and policymaking, partly due to the persistent “model minority” stereotype, which falsely suggests uniform success and obscures disparities within the group. These disparities include unequal healthcare access, gaps in health literacy, and underuse of preventive services.2 As a result, the AAPI population face increased vulnerability to preventable diseases such as cancer.3

Unlike most racial/ethnic groups in the United States, cancer surpasses heart disease as the leading cause of death among the AAPI population.1 Human papillomavirus (HPV), the most prevalent sexually transmitted infection, contributes significantly to this burden. The high prevalence of HPV among this population is especially concerning, as it is linked to cervical, anal, oropharyngeal, and other genital cancers that are largely preventable through vaccination. For maximum protection, the HPV vaccine requires a two-dose series for individuals who initiate vaccination before age 15, and a three-dose series for those who start at age 15 or older, as well as for immunocompromised individuals.4 HPV vaccination initiation is defined as receipt of at least one dose of the vaccine, while completion refers to receiving all recommended doses in the series.4 HPV vaccination intention is an individual’s willingness to be vaccinated against HPV and serves as an important indicator influencing vaccination decisions.5 Despite its proven effectiveness, HPV vaccination rates remain disproportionately low among the AAPI population. The National Health Interview Survey (2015–2018) shows that Asian American females initiate HPV vaccination at much lower rates (12.9%) compared with non-Hispanic White females (36.1%).6 Additional data show that Asian Americans aged 9–26 have the lowest HPV vaccine initiation rates nationwide.6 These gaps highlight the urgent need to understand cultural, linguistic, and structural barriers that shape vaccination decisions.

Contributing barriers to vaccination include cultural stigma and taboos surrounding sexual health, limited English proficiency, lack of culturally appropriate information, and structural challenges such as unfamiliarity with the healthcare system and insurance coverage.7 These factors contribute to persistent disparities despite the availability of a safe and effective vaccine.

This review was undertaken to address the significant public health concern of low HPV vaccination rates among AAPI population in the United States despite the availability of an effective and safe vaccine. We have previously published a full protocol.8 The AAPI population is disproportionately impacted by HPV-related cancers, making it crucial to understand the factors influencing vaccination rates in this population. This review synthesized existing evidence from descriptive and correlational studies, including qualitative, quantitative, and mixed-methods research on HPV vaccination within AAPI communities. This review identified key determinants, such as vaccination intention and perceived risk, that influence uptake. The findings provide valuable insights for developing culturally tailored interventions that reflect the AAPI population’s unique cultural beliefs, language preferences, healthcare access patterns, and community norms that shape their health behaviors. By pinpointing these culturally specific facilitators and barriers, the findings can guide the design of tailored intervention materials, communication strategies, and outreach approaches that are more likely to resonate with individuals across diverse AAPI subgroups and improve intervention effectiveness. Additionally, this review identifies gaps in the current literature and highlights the need for future research with stronger study designs to enhance the validity and generalizability of findings, thereby contributing to the broader knowledge base and informing targeted evidence-based public health strategies and policy efforts to reduce HPV-related health disparities in AAPI communities.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This scoping review and meta-analysis was conducted in accordance with PRISMA-P reporting guidelines.9 The scoping component of this review aimed to map the breadth and characteristics of existing research on HPV vaccination among the AAPI population. In contrast, the meta-analysis component quantitatively synthesized available data on vaccination initiation and completion rates to estimate pooled outcomes and examine patterns across studies, providing a more precise assessment of disparities among studies. A detailed protocol was published previously8 and registered with PROSPERO (CRD420251008431). Below, we provide a summary of the approach, noting minor updates where applicable.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p:
The scoping review and meta-analysis followed the methodological framework outlined in our published protocol.8 We included peer-reviewed, English-language studies using descriptive or correlational designs (e.g., cross-sectional surveys, observational studies, or mixed-methods research with extractable quantitative data). A PRISMA flow diagram (Figure 1) summarizes the study selection process. The review covered publications from inception through March 10, 2025; the review timeline spans from August 14, 2024, to March 10, 2025. Figure 1.
PRISMA flow diagram.

sec.title: Literature search
sec.p: Searches were performed in PubMed, CINAHL, PsycINFO, and Cochrane (see Supplementary Material). Screening occurred in two stages. In the first stage, two reviewers (SS and AF) independently assessed titles and abstracts. In the second stage, the same reviewers evaluated full texts, with additional independent review by ACC. To ensure quality control, 10% of exclusions were cross-checked by an additional reviewer (SM) (Figure 1).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Consistent with the protocol,8 we included studies that: (1) were published in English; (2) examined HPV vaccination intention (e.g., willingness, acceptability) or uptake (initiation or completion); (3) focused on the AAPI population in the U.S., with subgroup-specific data if drawn from a multiethnic sample; and (4) used descriptive, correlational, or mixed-methods designs. We excluded reviews, conference abstracts, trial registrations, protocols, dissertations, and intervention or randomized controlled trials. Intervention studies were excluded to maintain a focus on observational evidence regarding determinants of HPV vaccination rather than intervention efficacy.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data was extracted using a standardized extraction form developed in Microsoft Excel. Three reviewers (SM, SS and AF) independently extracted data using prespecified forms. Extracted information included publication details (authors, year), study population characteristics, design and theoretical frameworks, and key outcomes related to HPV vaccination (intention, initiation, and completion). Disagreements were resolved through discussion or consultation with a fourth reviewer (ACC).

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: Risk of bias was independently evaluated by three reviewers (SM, SS, ACC) using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) Checklist for observational studies. While our protocol had specified alternative tools, we applied the JBI checklist to better align with the descriptive and correlational designs of included studies. This tool provided a structured appraisal of methodological rigor, clarity, and reliability.10 Each study was rated across relevant domains, and scores were calculated based on the number of “Yes” responses (see Supplementary Material).

sec.title: Strategy for data synthesis
sec.p: We synthesized findings using both narrative and quantitative approaches. Qualitative and mixed-method results were synthesized thematically to contextualize quantitative findings. Descriptive findings were narratively summarized, highlighting patterns, themes, and key factors influencing HPV vaccination behaviors among the AAPI population. Quantitative data were combined to identify common variables, the direction of effects, and the strength of associations. When appropriate, findings from similar studies were grouped by study design, outcome type, or population subgroup. Results were presented in both tables and narrative form to offer a comprehensive overview of the evidence.

sec.title: Main outcomes
sec.p: The main outcomes in this review included HPV vaccination intention, uptake (initiation or completion of the vaccine series), and factors influencing these outcomes. These outcomes were prespecified in our published protocol8 and are summarized here with added clarification on how they were measured and synthesized in the current review.

sec.title: HPV vaccination uptake
sec.p: We defined uptake as the proportion of AAPI participants who had received at least one HPV vaccine dose (initiation) or completed the recommended two- or three-dose series (completion, depending on age and immune status). Data sources included self-reported surveys, interviews (e.g., yes/no questions, number of doses received), and medical records or vaccination registries when available. The prevalence of uptake was summarized as percentages, with time frames varying by study (e.g., vaccination status at survey date or within the prior year[s]).

sec.title: HPV vaccination intention
sec.p: Vaccination intention reflected willingness or likelihood to receive the HPV vaccine in the future. This variable was typically measured using survey items or standardized questionnaires (e.g., “How likely are you to get the HPV vaccine?”). Some studies measured broader vaccine acceptance using scales that captured attitudes toward vaccination. In this review, we summarized intention as the proportion of participants indicating a positive or high likelihood of getting vaccinated, based on responses collected at the time of each study.

sec.title: Knowledge and awareness of HPV and Vaccination
sec.p: We also examined level of knowledge and awareness about HPV and the vaccine among the AAPI population.

sec.title: Perceived risk of HPV
sec.p: This variable reflected the perceived personal risk of contracting HPV, which has been shown to affect vaccination rates. This may include perceived vulnerability to HPV-related cancers or infections.

sec.title: Barriers and facilitators to vaccination
sec.p: These variables referred to factors that hindered or facilitated HPV vaccination among the AAPI population. These can include cultural, social, or healthcare-related factors (e.g., stigma, language barriers, access to health care) that shaped vaccination decision-making.

sec.title: Meta-analysis
sec.p: Where quantitative data permitted, we extracted various effect estimates related to HPV vaccination intention or uptake, including correlation coefficients, regression coefficients, odds ratios (ORs), relative risks (RRs), or risk differences, along with their corresponding measures of uncertainty, such as 95% confidence intervals (CIs). A meta-analysis was performed using random-effects models to account for potential heterogeneity across studies for each type of factor. Quantitative synthesis was restricted to analyses in which studies reported the same type of effect estimate. If the data were found to be highly heterogeneous or too limited to support meta-analysis, we instead summarized the study findings graphically and narratively, following the guidance in Chapter 12 of the Cochrane Handbook on synthesizing and presenting findings using other methods.11 Additionally, we examined the direction and consistency of each association of interest across studies to better characterize patterns in HPV vaccination intention and uptake among the AAPI population. All meta-analyses were performed using STATA version 19 (College Station, TX: StataCorp LLC).

sec.title: Study selection and characteristics
sec.p:
A total of 3474 records were identified through database searching, of which 675 duplicates were removed, leaving 2799 records for screening. After title and abstract review, 2705 records were excluded, and 93 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility. Of these, 51 were excluded for reasons including unsuitable study design, outcomes, or populations. Ultimately, 42 studies met inclusion criteria and were incorporated into the final scoping review (Figure 1). Studies included in this review were published between 2010 and 2024, covering diverse AAPI communities across the United States. Most studies used quantitative cross-sectional designs (n = 29), followed by mixed-methods (n = 11), and qualitative studies (n = 2). Sample sizes varied from small pilot groups of 5–50 participants to large datasets exceeding 100,000 individuals. The majority of studies focused on women and mothers, with a few including adolescents, young adults, or parent-child dyads. Most studies were conducted in urban U.S. settings with high AAPI population density (Table 1).Table 1.
Characteristics of studies and research Question and study aims.
Author, YearStudy Sample Size & Age RangeStudy with Various Race/Ethnicity & Asian/API Sample Size & Age RangeAAPI GroupResearch Question(s)/Study Aim(s)
Ackerson et al. 201712Sample size: 18,800; Age range: 9–17 (12.6 ± 2.22)Yes; Asian/API sample: 6078Asian Americans/APIs (group not specified)Examine trends and correlates of HPV vaccine completion in boys before and after ACIP recommendation.
Agénor et al., 202013N = 3536; Age range: 18–31Yes; Asian n = 231 (5.41%); Age: 18–32U.S. Asian women aged 18–31Magnitude and mechanisms of racial/ethnic disparities in initiating/completing HPV vaccine.
Agénor et al., 201814Total N = 15,502; Age 18–31Yes; Asian N = 1,066 (U.S born 393, foreign born 673)Foreign-born Asian WomenExamine HPV vaccine initiation by nativity status and race/ethnicity.
Akpan et al., 202415N = 15,863; Age 27–45Yes; Non-Hispanic Asian n = 573 (6.7%)Non-Hispanic AsiansAssess HPV vaccination among U.S. adults aged 27–45 and factors for initiation after age 26.
Bastani et al., 201116N = 490; Ethnic minority girls in LA CountyYes; Chinese 20% (N = 98), Korean 13.5% (N = 66)Chinese American, Korean AmericanAssess uptake and correlates among low-income, ethnic minority girls in LA County.
Beltran et al., 201617N = 192; Female Hmong Americans (mean age 29.24)No explicit race/ethnicity comparison mentionedHmong AmericansInvestigate HPV knowledge, vaccination rates, and factors linked to literacy in Hmong community.

Lee et al., 202133N = 4,602; Age 18–26Yes; Asian American/APIs = 329; Age 18–26Cambodian American mothers & daughtersUnderstand awareness, knowledge, social norms, and health communication.
Lu et al., 201534N = 171 Asian Indian parents of children aged 9–16Not reportedNon-Hispanic AsianAssess adult vaccination rates by race/ethnicity in U.S.

Ratnasamy & Chagpar, 202242N = 8,436; Age 9–26Yes; Asian American/APIs = 816 (various subgroups)Indian ancestryDetermine vaccination rates among POIA in the U.S.
Taylor et al., 201243N = 32 Interviews; N = 408 Survey; Mothers age 39–53, children 9–18NoCambodian womenAddress barriers, facilitators, and uptake for mothers with daughters 9–18.
Taylor et al., 201444N = 188,250 adults; Age 19 to ≥65Yes; Asian American/APIs: Chinese 3165; Indian 3525; Filipino 3656; other Asian 5819; Age 19 to ≥65Cambodian mothersProvide info for educational materials/interventions.
Truong-Vu, 202445N = 113; Mean age 22Not reportedNL Southeast Asian AmericanInvestigate disaggregated data use and implications for targeting interventions.
Vu et al., 2022a46N = 165; Age 25–45 (median 38)NoU.S. Vietnamese parentsExplore practice-, provider-, patient-level determinants using P3 model.
Vu et al., 2022b47N = 32 Interview; N = 408 Survey; Mothers 39–53; Children 9–18No; Asian/API: 32 Interview; 408 Survey; Mothers 39–53; Children 9–18U.S. Vietnamese MothersUnderstand multilevel influences on mothers’ decision-making for adolescents.
Vu et al., 2022b47408 survey participants; 32 interview participants; Mothers 39–53; Children 9–18No; Asian/API: 408 survey; 32 interview; Mothers 39–53; Children 9–18U.S. Vietnamese parentsAssess trusted sources, perceived message effectiveness, preferred dissemination.
Wang et al., 20236,n = 188,250 (non-Hispanic White adults, n = 172,085); Age 19 to ≥65Yes; Asian/API: Chinese (3,165), Asian Indian (3,525), Filipino (3,656), other Asian (5,819); Age 19 to ≥65Asian AmericanAssess vaccination rates for key vaccines among disaggregated AAPI subgroups.
Yi et al., 201348N = 113; Mean age 22Not reportedVietnamese-American WomenDetermine receipt and whether proficiency and knowledge are associated.
Zhao et al., 201449N = 165; Age 25–45; median 38 (M = 36.7, SD = 6.1)No; Asian/API: 83; Age 25–45Korean American WomenExamine determinants of intention to vaccinate daughters; explore self-construal’s role.
Zhao et al., 202350N = 5567; Age 9–17Yes; Asian/API: 268; Age 9–17Asian AmericansCompare initiation/completion rates between Asian Americans and peers.
Zhu et al., 201951N = 110 (75 M, 35 F Chinese-American parents/guardians); Child age 11–18; Parent age N/RNo; Asian/API: 110; Age 11–18Chinese in Greater PhiladelphiaAssess knowledge, attitudes, beliefs, factors associated with willingness.

The studies included in this review represented a range of AAPI subgroups, although representation was uneven. Chinese, Korean, and Vietnamese populations were the most frequently studied. Broader, unspecified AAPI groups were also common in large national datasets. Other subgroups, including Cambodian, Filipino, Hmong, Asian Indian, and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander populations, were represented less frequently, typically in smaller or community-based studies (Table 2).Table 2.
Representation of Asian American and Pacific Islander subgroups across included HPV vaccination studies.
AAPI SubgroupNumber of StudiesExample Studies/Notes
Chinese56,16,37,44,51
Korean516,26,28,31,49
Vietnamese622,24,30,46–48
Cambodian430,33,43,44
Hmong117
Filipino36,20,44
Asian Indian/Indian ancestry56,34,36,42,44
Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander118
Broad/unspecified Asian/API8+12,19,21,29,32,39,41,50
Other Southeast Asian (non-labeled)145
Notes: “Number of studies” counts entries where the subgroup is explicitly studied; some large national datasets include multiple subgroups under “broad/unspecified Asian”.

sec.title: JBI quality analysis
sec.p:
Across the included studies, quantitative cross-sectional studies had an average JBI critical appraisal score of 59.3%, indicating moderate methodological quality. Variability largely due to unclear reporting of confounding factors and measurement validity (Table 3). Lower-scoring studies (i.e., Kharbanda et al.25; Lee et al.31) demonstrated unclear or incomplete handling of confounding factors and statistical analysis, underscoring common challenges in cross-sectional designs. Qualitative studies showed a slightly higher average score of about 91%, indicating strong alignment between methodology and research aims, although some studies (i.e., Bastani et al.16; Hopfer et al.24) lacked complete reflexivity or full representation of participant voices (Table 4).Table 3.
Joanna Briggs Institute checklist for quantitative studies with scores.

jConclusions Flow from Data Analysis.

sec.title: HPV vaccination uptake initiation and completion
sec.p: Multiple studies have explored HPV vaccination initiation and completion rates among the AAPI population. For example, Ackerson et al.12 examined series completion before and after recommendations for boys. Agénor et al.13,14 investigated racial and ethnic disparities in vaccination initiation and completion among U.S. Asian women. Akpan et al.15 and Adjei Boakye et al.18 reported prevalence data on NHPI adults. Additional research by Freeman et al.,21 Hechter et al.,23 Kharbanda et al.,25 Kim et al.,27 Zhao et al.,50 Truong-Vu,45 and Nomura & Rahman38 provided insights into vaccination patterns among various Asian subgroups. Large surveys, such as those by McElfish et al.35 and Lu et al.,34 highlighted disparities based on race, nativity, and sex. Agénor et al.13 found that only 35% of Asian American young women had initiated the vaccine series, with fewer than 20% completing it. Foreign-born Asians have much lower odds of initiation and completion compared to U.S.-born Whites, with ORs ranging from 0.25 to 0.43.

sec.title: Sociodemographic and nativity factors
sec.p: Several studies found that sociodemographic factors, including age, sex/gender, education, income, and nativity (U.S.-born vs. foreign-born), significantly impact HPV vaccination rates.14,21,34,35,39,50 Foreign-born AAPI individuals often have lower vaccination rates than U.S.-born individuals due to barriers such as language, cultural beliefs, and limited familiarity with preventive care in the U.S. healthcare system. In particular, Beltran et al.17 highlight the unique cultural factors influencing vaccination decisions among Hmong Americans.

sec.title: Awareness and knowledge
sec.p: Knowledge gaps were a recurring theme in this systematic review and meta-analysis. Studies such as Cui et al.,19 Beltran et al.,17 Lee et al.,29,31,32 Yi et al.,48 Nguyen et al.,37 and Zhu et al.51 highlighted limited awareness and knowledge about HPV and its vaccine among AAPI subgroups, including Hmong, Vietnamese, Korean, and Chinese Americans. Dela Cruz et al.20 and Nuño et al.40 similarly identified gaps in HPV literacy among parents and students.

sec.title: Cultural beliefs and barriers
sec.p: Factors affecting uptake included structural, social, and individual factors. Taylor et al.,43,44 Vu et al.,46,47 and Nonzee et al.39 described parental barriers, trusted information sources, and decision-making patterns among Cambodian and Vietnamese parents. Mullassery & Posmontier36 and Ratnasamy and Chagpar,42 examined cultural beliefs and acculturation in Asian Indian communities. Cultural norms, social expectations, and language were frequently reported influences across Korean, Chinese, and Southeast Asian subgroups.26,28,33,49,50 Studies also emphasized the importance of culturally tailored messaging.24,47

sec.title: Healthcare access and provider communication
sec.p: Several studies highlighted the importance of healthcare access, insurance coverage, and provider recommendations in increasing HPV vaccine uptake among AAPI communities. Lack of provider recommendations was identified as a major barrier.41,443953 Some research emphasized that culturally sensitive communication and language-appropriate materials are crucial for improving vaccination rates.36,54,55 Importantly, access to trusted community clinics and bilingual healthcare providers helped facilitate vaccination.42,47

sec.title: Meta-analysis findings
sec.p:
We conducted meta-analyses for studies with extractable quantitative data. The pooled prevalence estimates among the AAPI population were 41% (22%–61%) for HPV vaccine awareness (Figure 2), 48% (6%–91%) for vaccine acceptance (Figure 3), 43% (33%–54%) for vaccine intention (Figure 4), 26% (21%–32%) for vaccine initiation (Figure 5), and 21% (13%–30%) for vaccine completion (Figure 6). When comparing the AAPI population with non-AAPI population the pooled OR was 0.76 (0.67–0.87) for HPV vaccine initiation (Figure 7) and 0.67 (0.51–0.86) for vaccine completion (Figure 8), indicating that the AAPI population were less likely to initiate or complete HPV vaccination. Additionally, pooled OR estimates from regression models showed that the AAPI population had a lower likelihood of vaccine uptake compared with Whites (pooled OR: 0.75 [0.63–0.90]; Figure 9). All pooled analyses demonstrated substantial heterogeneity, as reflected in I2 values in the respective figures. Figure 2.
Pooled point prevalence HPV vaccine awareness among AAPIs. Figure 3.
Pooled point prevalence of HPV vaccine acceptance among AAPIs. Figure 4.
Pooled point prevalence of HPV vaccine intention among AAPIs. Figure 5.
Pooled point prevalence of HPV vaccine initiation among AAPIs. Figure 6.
Pooled point prevalence of HPV vaccine completion among AAPIs. Figure 7.
Pooled odds ratio for HPV vaccine initiation: AAPIs vs. non-AAPIs. Figure 8.
Pooled odds ratio for HPV vaccine completion: AAPIs vs. non-AAPIs. Figure 9.
Pooled odds ratio from regression analysis of HPV vaccine uptake: AAPIs vs. Whites.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This scoping review and meta-analysis examined the complex HPV vaccination patterns among the AAPI population. It shows persistent disparities in both initiation and completion of the vaccine series. These gaps are shaped by various intersecting factors such as limited awareness, cultural beliefs, healthcare access obstacles, and broader sociodemographic influences.

Although HPV vaccination coverage has generally improved in the United States, AAPI communities continue to have lower vaccination initiation and series completion rates than other racial and ethnic groups.12,14,34 Persistent barriers include lack of awareness and its role in cancer prevention, particularly among immigrant populations who may not be familiar with HPV-related diseases or the purpose of the vaccine.17,37 These findings underscore the need for culturally and linguistically responsive educational interventions tailored to diverse AAPI subgroups.

Consistent with prior research, access to healthcare, insurance coverage, and clear provider recommendations emerged as crucial facilitators of vaccination.39,41 Provider endorsement remains one of the strongest predictors of vaccination. However, communication between providers and AAPI patients is often inconsistent, hindered by language barriers or cultural misunderstandings that can compromise patient-provider interactions.36,44

Cultural and social norms also play significant roles in shaping vaccination choices. Factors such as stigma related to sexual health, parental discomfort in discussing sexually transmitted infections, and adherence to traditional beliefs influence attitudes toward HPV vaccination.28,47 Addressing these cultural nuances highlight the need of engaging trusted community and faith community leaders, community health workers, cultural brokers to close knowledge gaps and normalize vaccination discussions.

The impact of sociodemographic and nativity factors is notable. Foreign-born AAPI individuals are less likely than their U.S.-born counterparts to start or complete the HPV vaccine series.34,35 Factors such as lower education and income, limited awareness of preventive healthcare, and language barriers intensify these disparities. Future public health initiatives should address these interconnected social determinants of health, acknowledging the diversity within AAPI communities and avoiding a one-size-fits-all strategy.

Additionally, the persistent gap between knowledge and HPV vaccination behavior highlights that increasing knowledge and awareness alone is insufficient to change vaccination behaviors. Factors such as acculturation level influence how families interpret health information and make vaccination decisions.56 For instance, newer immigrants may support culturally familiar healthcare practices, while later-generation individuals may be more influenced by societal and/or peer norms.57 Furthermore, stigma surrounding sexual health, especially among girls, may discourage open discussions with providers, and intergenerational expectations often require parental or even extended-family approval before pursuing preventive care.58 Socioeconomic constraints further intersect with linguistic and navigational challenges. Therefore, families with lower incomes or recent immigrant status may experience greater structural barriers despite being motivated. Addressing these multi-layered influences requires strategies that go beyond general references to “traditional beliefs” and instead recognize how gender norms, generational position, cultural expectations, and economic context shape vaccination behaviors. Policymakers and healthcare systems should prioritize culturally and linguistically responsive services, and researchers should continue to disaggregate AAPI subgroup data to uncover distinct needs that remain obscured in aggregated analyses.

In addition to these behavioral and sociocultural barriers, recent molecular and immunological research offers further insight into HPV vaccination disparities among the AAPI population. Recent studies examining immune response mechanisms and HPV vaccine efficacy across genetically and environmentally diverse populations suggest that biological factors may interact with cultural and behavioral determinants of vaccine hesitancy. Advances in immuno-oncology provide a deeper understanding of how the tumor immune microenvironment shapes HPV-associated cancer development. New tools such as the HPV-associated Tumor Immune MicroEnvironment ExploreR (HPVTIMER) allow systematic exploration of immune pathways in HPV-associated cancers and may inform precision-intervention strategies.59 Integrating these molecular perspectives strengthens the theoretical basis for developing precision-informed interventions to consider biological variability alongside sociocultural contexts within AAPI communities. These insights are not proposed as causal explanations for vaccination disparities but may help inform future intervention strategies when aligned with cultural, behavioral, and healthcare system – level determinants.

The quality appraisal highlights important considerations for interpreting the methodological findings. Quantitative cross-sectional studies demonstrated moderate methodological quality, with an average JBI score of 59.3%, and variability largely driven by incomplete handling of confounding factors and limitations in measurement validity. Studies such as Lee et al.31 and Kharbanda et al.25 exemplified these challenges, reflecting common issues in cross-sectional designs where causal inferences are limited and statistical adjustments are inconsistently applied. In contrast, qualitative studies generally exhibited higher methodological rigor, averaging 91%, indicating strong alignment between research aims and methods. However, some studies, including Bastani et al.16 and Hopfer et al.24 demonstrated gaps in reflexivity and full representation of participant perspectives, highlighting areas where interpretive depth could be strengthened. Together, these findings underscore the need for future research to employ more robust and transparent methodologies, including careful consideration of confounding variables in quantitative designs and enhanced reflexivity and participant engagement in qualitative work. Strengthening methodological rigor will be critical for producing reliable and actionable insights into HPV vaccination behaviors in AAPI populations.

Analysis of the dataset indicates that confounding factors were predominantly acknowledged and appropriately addressed across the included studies. A substantial majority (28 of 37) identified confounding factors and reported addressing them. Conversely, studies that did not identify confounding factors (n = 4) generally did not address them, with three reporting no action taken and one presenting an unclear response. This alignment indicates that failure to identify confounders is typically associated with an absence of mitigation strategies, which may introduce a risk of residual confounding and potential bias. For studies in which confounding identification was rated as unclear (n = 4), responses varied: three reported dealing with confounders despite the ambiguity, while one did not. This inconsistency may reflect differences in reporting quality or variability in how confounding assessment criteria were interpreted.

The wide variation in sample sizes across studies also has important implications for the robustness of the overall findings. Smaller samples are more susceptible to random error, low statistical power, and instability of effect estimates, which can lead to exaggerated or unreliable results. In contrast, very large samples can produce highly precise estimates but may also detect statistically significant effects that are too small to be practically meaningful. Additionally, differences in population characteristics (e.g., demographic composition or contextual factors) may further contribute to heterogeneity that influences both the direction and magnitude of observed effects. Together, these variations may limit the comparability of studies and complicate the synthesis of results, underscoring the need for careful interpretation and, where possible, the use of methods that account for heterogeneity such as sensitivity analyses or meta-analytic weighting.

This review has limitations. Certain AAPI subgroups remain underrepresented, which may obscure meaningful within-group differences. Additionally, some studies relied on self-reported vaccination status, which can lead to recall or social desirability bias. Studies published in Asian languages or based in small community contexts may also have been missed, potentially limiting representation of locally relevant insights. Despite these limitations, the findings point to important directions for future research and practice. Future research should focus on disaggregating AAPI subgroups and adopt stronger designs to enhance validity and generalizability. Partnerships with community organizations are critical for developing and testing interventions that reflect cultural and linguistic contexts. Addressing provider communication gaps and ensuring access to affordable and culturally responsive care are also essential to reducing HPV-related disparities in the AAPI population.

Based on these findings, targeted policy and clinical strategies are needed. Policymakers should implement culturally and linguistically responsive programs, promote provider training to enhance communication with diverse AAPI patients, and integrate HPV vaccination into routine preventive care.60 Clinically, interventions should consider family decision-making dynamics, gender and generational influences, and socioeconomic barriers. Moreover, emerging genomic and immunological research offers opportunities to develop individualized vaccine promotion strategies that account for genetic background and immune response variability, potentially increasing vaccine efficacy and acceptance. Integrating these multi-level, precision-informed approaches can support equitable HPV vaccination uptake across the diverse AAPI population.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Significant disparities in HPV vaccination persist among the AAPI population, driven by multiple intersecting factors including nativity, cultural and linguistic influences, healthcare access barriers, socioeconomic constraints, and variations in provider communication. Culturally and linguistically responsive strategies, including digital storytelling and other tailored interventions, hold promise for increasing vaccine uptake and reducing HPV-related cancer burden in the AAPI population. Sustained efforts to address both structural and cultural barriers are warranted to achieve equitable vaccination coverage and cancer prevention.7,10,61–63

bio.p: Angela Chia-Chen Chen, PHD, PMHNP-BC, is a Nurse Practitioner at MSU Health Care Family Medicine. Dr. Chen received her doctorate degree of nursing science from the University of Washington and completed her post-master’s Psychiatric and Mental Health Nurse Practitioner at the University of Washington. Dr. Chen has also served as a visiting professor with University of California-San Francisco’s Center for AIDS prevention studies. Most recently she served as a psychiatric mental health nurse practitioner at the Crisis Preparation and Recovery in Tempe, Arizona. In addition to her clinical practice, Dr. Chen serves as McLaren Greater Lansing Endowed Chair for Behavioral Mental Health Nursing Professor in MSU’s College of Nursing Education and Research. Dr. Chen’s main areas of interest include the promotion of HIV vaccinations, combating health disparities, and focusing on the mental health of minority youth through cross-cultural research.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Supplementary material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed online at https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2026.2613575

sec.title: Disclaimer
sec.p: This research did not include any studies involving animal or human participants, nor did it occur in any private or protected area. Therefore, no specific ethical permission was required to conduct this review.
